page.text: Clearance of pipes underneath steel beams with and without insulation ................................... 15
Annex E Orientation of piping at columns, vertical vessels and tanks ......................................................... 23
This General Engineering Specification (GES) applies to the design and construction of industrial and chemical
plants. It covers technical details and requirements for the design and routing of aboveground steel piping.
Layout and piping shall be designed in compliance with the following basic documents:
Statutory rules & regulations of central & local governing bodies e.g. Factories Act, OISD, Central & State
Good engineering practices for plant operation and Maintenance.
HPL Painting specification (DOC ID :: IP/G/GEN/IS_PAINT , Rev-03)
Piping design shall be in accordance with documents listed in Table 2.

text.b: References to be defined within project
Doc ID Code of project-specific ES

page.text: Process and utility piping shall be designed in accordance with ASME B31.3 and documents listed in section 3.
Applicable design conditions and components to be used for piping design are specified in the Project Piping
Line List and Project Piping Class Specification.
Critical piping is subject to a piping stress analyses. Criterions are specified in ES Piping Stress Analyses.
The locations and types of tie-in points of aboveground pipelines shall be determined early and referenced in
overall plot plan or separate tie-in plan.
Piping shall be laid such that pipe runs are cleanly arranged. Pipe runs shall be as short as possible while
accounting for flexibility requirements. The number of fittings and supporting structures shall be limited to the
minimum. Unnecessary high points and low points shall be avoided.
On gas and vapor lines low points (pockets) where liquid condensate can accumulate are not allowed.
Horizontal piping shall not be sloped unless indicated in the P&I diagrams.
Maintenance and inspection requirements have to be considered. Free escape routes and access to the plant
units and equipment must be ensured in compliance with the escape and rescue route requirements. (See
The minimum clearance of drains between the lower edge of the flange and the upper edge of the floor / platform
shall be in accordance with Annex A.
Pipe clearance from steel structures is specified in Annex B.
Dead ends of headers shall be closed with blind flanges along with Suitable drain/ vent closer to end flange. or
Flanges, according to pipe-class, shall only be used for connections to process vessels, machines, valves or
measuring orifices. Additional flange assemblies can be required:
to separate pressure test systems in case of spec break or PMS changes.
for required removal of piping components (e.g. for tongue and groove or RTJ flange connections or conical
on piping systems or sections with welded valves (SW) for break-out connections. (e.g. small diameter by-
All small bore piping connected to pump (like OWS, CBD, Seal/Gland Leakage etc) for removal of pump.
allow adequate flushing or purging of lines,
provide high and low point drains for hydrostatic testing,
Center-to-center distances for steel pipelines shall be selected in accordance with Annex D to ensure clearance
(steel or insulation) during operation and installation. Insulation thickness depends on the operation temperature
Piping design shall also consider neighboring lines. Lines in process and utility units running in the same
direction shall be supported together. For this reason piping layout shall anticipate suitable pipe traces with
bottom of pipe / bottom of pipe shoe on the same elevation for a common and supporting structure.
A minimum headroom of 2100 mm shall apply above walkways. Low level and potential trip hazard lines shall
not be arranged across access or escape ways. These access ways shall not be obstructed by any piping or
equipment components. This does not apply to process-related minimum distance requirements of equipment
(such as columns, reboilers, etc.). The minimum widths and heights requirements are specified in ES Plant
For pipelines smaller than DN 40 / NPS 1 1/2" no isometric will be issued. Sketches extracted from model will
be issued for routing guidance to site execution contractor. Field routing and field supporting on basis of a bill
of material shall apply. (further detail shall be referred as per Technical proposal, Rev.05 clause 2.3)
The design of piping connected to equipment shall allow access to equipment components for operation,
Piping shall allow the removal of rotating equipment and motors/drivers (no piping directly above machines and
Additional recommendations for piping at machinery are listed in Chapter 6 of API 686, and PIP REIE686A.
Eccentric reducers in pump suction lines shall be oriented as:
for slurry service: flat side bottom.
The length of the straight piece of pipe from the last elbow to the suction nozzle including reducers shall be
sufficient to ensure minimum flow turbulence at the pump suction. If strainers or valves are installed downstream
of the elbow, the straight length is to be determined downstream of such flow-influencing components (full port
gate and ball valves are not necessarily flow-influencing components). Refer pump manufacturer requirement
if specified in general arrangement drawing.

text.b: Straight length requirements on pump suction lines

page.text: Elbow in the same plane as the pump shaft
Elbow in a plane perpendicular to the pump shaft
Elbow in a plane perpendicular to the pump shaft
Elbow in the same plane as the pump shaft
Elbow in a plane perpendicular to the pump shaft
1) D= diameter of pump suction nozzle
2) Measures how to prevent the impact of unequal distribution have to be clarified with the pump manufacturer.
3) Straight length from vendor to be followed if it is maximum than min. specified straight length in this table.
All water piping and pump cooling jackets shall be arranged for complete drain ability.
For top suction, top discharge pumps, sufficient space shall be left in front of pump for removal of pump
Piping shall be designed to provide clearance for pump driver removal while the suction and discharge valves
Suction lines of gas compressors downstream of drains or liquid separators (as shown in the P&I diagrams)
have to be designed to prevent liquid from entering into the machine, i.e. the machine shall be the high point. If
due to layout reasons a continuous slope away from the machine is not possible, a separate process drain into
a closed system has to be provided at the low point.
Process piping & support at reciprocating compressors shall be designed to ensure
balanced gas distribution to the cylinders,
compliance with the requirements of the pulsation study.
Lines shall not be connected to the dead ends of compressor headers.
In horizontal suction lines, “flat bottom” type eccentric reducers shall be used to prevent the accumulation of
condensate. Suitable drain to be provided in case of free draining is not possible.
Valves shall be connected directly to vessel or column flanges.
Outlet nozzles and piping on vertical vessels/Column shall be try to oriented as specified in Annex E.
On vertical vessels with skirts flange connection shall be located outside of the skirt.
The positions and orientation of nozzles, lifting lugs and nameplates shall be determined and marked-up on
Lifting lugs shall be arranged to ensure free turning of the (lifting) rope around the lugs when lifting the equipment
from the horizontal into the vertical position. This is an important design criterion especially in case the column
piping and platforms shall be installed in the horizontal column position at ground level (pre-dressing)
Lines at shell and tube heat exchangers shall be arranged and supported in such a way that heads and bundles
can be removed. To implement this requirement additional break flanges can be provided. Where the bundles
can be pulled, the routing shall consider space to allow for the use of a tube bundle extractor (see ES Plant
Fixed tube heat exchangers which are cleaned with high pressure jet lances require a free space equal to the
tube length plus at least 1 m.
If shell and tube heat exchangers are designated to be taken out for cleaning as a whole, then no lines shall run
above the exchanger in order to facilitate disassembly.
Lines connected to plate-type heat exchangers shall be arranged in such a way that maintenance access for
the removal of the plates is available.
Piping connected to top access filters shall be routed to ensure removal of baskets or cartridges.
In horizontal pipe runs larger than DN 50 / NPS 2” Y-type strainers shall be installed with filter leg turned
maximum 45° sideward in order to have better access for maintenance (see Annex A).
The following requirements shall be met when connecting piping to burners:
Access ways to the burners for maintenance and cleaning work and emergency exits shall not be
Burner piping shall be routed uniform and symmetrically providing unobstructed access to the inspection
doors and peepholes. valves to be operable while viewing from peepholes
Breakout flanges shall be provided in the fuel lines at minimum distance to the burners.
Only aboveground pipelines which are related to the tank shall be located within the bunded area.
Pipelines which penetrate the bund wall should be limited to pump suction lines. These pipelines shall be
provided with an external protection pipe. The annular space between pipeline and pipe sleeve shall be sealed.
All other lines such as tank filling lines and firefighting lines should run over the bund wall.
Steam supply and condensate return lines shall be connected to the top of the headers. Block valves shall be
arranged in the horizontal run just outside of the pipe rack. Operating accessibility for the block valve to be
All steam lines shall be routed to achieve a sufficient number of steam traps while ensuring a proper
condensate removal. Steam lines must be drained immediately upstream of any vertical risers and at dead
ends; see Annex F. Each drain connection has to be equipped with its own steam trap. Traps discharging to the
atmosphere shall have lines running to the nearest drain or sump.
Straight pipe sections for superheated steam shall be equipped with drains at intervals of 100 m and for
saturated steam at intervals of 50 m.
Steam traps shall be installed so that maintenance access is provided.
Utility stations shall preferably be located at main building columns or at pipe rack columns and at grade level
taking the maintenance requirements into account. All covered equipment shall be within the reach of the
The design of utility stations shall follow Annex G. For elevated utility stations at vertical vessels, columns and
in equipment structures provision of steam, plant air and nitrogen is usually sufficient.
The drain of the steam line shall be located at the lowest point of the vertical riser and to be equipped with a
steam trap if freezing cannot be excluded.
One complete set of utility hoses shall be provided for a number of 4 utility stations. Storage of hoses is foreseen

text.b: 5.2.3 Fuel gas, plant air, instrument air and nitrogen

page.text: All branches shall be taken from the top of the header. Block valves shall be arranged in the horizontal run just
All lines shall be routed with a minimum of low points. Low points in plant air and fuel gas lines have to be
drained (condensate formation) or to be heat traced.
The minimum nominal diameter of Pipe rack is DN 50 / NPS 2", unless otherwise specified by process.
Piping sleeper ways are the preferred supporting structure for interconnecting piping. Routing shall be on
common elevation to allow supporting on concrete sleepers (min. height 300 mm). Drains shall be grouped
The routing of piping on pipe racks, especially on multi-level racks, shall:
Lines of large nominal diameters shall be placed close to the pipe rack edge
Condensate lines shall be located on a lower level (backpressure steam traps)
Utility lines and lines carrying gases shall be placed on the upper level.
Lines carrying corrosive fluids & process lines shall be placed on the lowest level.
Cable Trays shall be placed on top tier.
Flanged connections and valves should not be located above roads,cable trays and ducts.
For design of pipe supports the priorities are:
1. Incorporate pipe supports, especially secondary supports into steel structure
4. Design special supports directly welded to pipe, e.g. trunnion supports
(The use of trunnion supports, especially on elbows, should be avoided due to complex
Pipe support design shall also consider neighboring lines. Lines in process units running in parallel shall be
supported together. Piping layout shall anticipate routing traces with bottom of pipe/shoe on the same elevation.
Uninsulated CS lines will be supported directly by the steel structure (from NPS >24” with a saddle plate).
Guidelines for selecting standard pipe supports including dimensions, allowable loads, restraint function and
materials are given in the Project Standard Pipe Support Specification.
The maximum allowable spacing of supports is specified in Annex C.
Due to supporting concern at drain / vent & similar cases, requirement of gusset / stiffener supporting of small
bore lines especially pulsating lines, pressure tappings, higher class lines etc. to be ensured.
Heights and orientation for operating valves are shown in Annex H.
The minimum spacing between hand wheels shall be 80 mm.
Elevated process valves DN 300 / NPS 12” and smaller can be operated by chain wheels. Arrangement shall
be as shown in Annex H. No chain operators shall be used for valves DN 25 / NPS 1” and smaller.
Chain operator devices shall neither block walkways nor hinder the operation of other valves.
However, portable / fixed platform can be planned based on requirement by review comments if any.
Generally the valves are hand wheel or lever operated. Gear operation shall be provided as under :
For wafer and lug type valves, unhindered turning of the disk (100 % opening) shall be ensured.
This means that components which could encroach on the free cross-sectional area of the line should not be
installed in the disk swiveling range (provide spacers rings where appropriate). Wafer type valves are not
allowed as end valves and shall not directly connected to equipment.
Block valves upstream or downstream of safety valves shall be full bore type. In case gate valve types the stems
shall be oriented horizontally. Only if this is not feasible due to routing, the stem could be oriented downward to
a maximum of 45° from the horizontal to keep the gate from falling off and blocking the flow.
Valves for normal operation shall be located so that they can be accessed and operated from grade or from a
platform. Valves DN 25 / NPS 1’’ and smaller may be operated from a permanent ladder (see ES Plant Layout).
Branch line block valves may be located in pipe racks, but consideration shall be given for access from adjacent
Control valves shall be installed in horizontal position with actuator facing upwards. Deviations are shown on
Clearance shall be provided above the control valve to remove the diaphragm or motor (min. 300 mm / 12”).
Arrangement (e.g. straight length requirements), sizes and dimensions, type of piping connections, scope of
supply for piping connection components shall be in compliance as per Instrumentation Arrangement and
Local instruments shall be visible and legible. All instrumentation items shall be designed to allow removal.
Drains and vents required for operational purposes (process drains and vents) shall be installed as shown in
the P&I diagram (see Annex I). If due to piping layout requirements the installation of additional process-related
drains and vents becomes necessary, they have to be marked up in the P&ID.
Drains and vents for pressure testing shall comply with Annex I. They are not shown in the P&I diagrams.
Existing flanged connections at high points of pipes of up to DN 150 / 6
can be used as pressure-test vents.
Vent connections shall run vertically upwards to safe location (see section 5.12.1).
For design and position of the sample connections refer detail Instrumentation Arrangement and Connection
The sample connection shall always tap off the main flow line with continuous flow and never at sections with
Insulation types shall be taken from the P&I diagrams and piping lists. Insulation thicknesses are specified in
insulation tables according to ES Insulation.
Piping design should allow for space for the required insulation. See Annex D.
If defined on the P&IDs personal protection shall be applied wherever a pipe can be reached. Personal
protection shall be installed up to 3 m above platform level.
Heat tracing systems shall be designed in accordance with the P&I diagrams and heat tracing specifications.
Electrical tracing shall be used for CPU/OCU plant.
In order to meet the requirements for lightning protection, protection, against electrical. shock and for explosion
protection conductive pipes shall be connected to the plant earthing system.

text.b: 5.11.1 Uncoated or unpainted steel piping systems

page.text: On these pipe systems all piping components are, by design, connected electrically conductive, e.g. flange
bolting, to each other. For this reason no special piping design requirements need to be followed. The
connection to the plant earthing system will be done via tensioning straps supplied and installed by the electrical

text.b: 5.11.2 Coated or painted steel piping systems

page.text: As on these piping systems it is not ensured that all piping components are always electrically conductive
connected to each other the following requirements apply:
Electrically conductive gaskets shall be specified, e.g. graphite filled spiral wound gaskets
Earthing lugs shall be welded to piping when lines are entering or leaving a closed building. The lugs shall
be placed outside the building structure. On horizontal lines they shall be oriented vertically down. On
vertical lines the lugs shall be directed to the nearest main steel structure member.
On interconnecting pipe racks earthing lugs shall be placed approx. every 30 m near a pipe rack axis/vertical
At electrical driven pumps on both, suction and discharge side, earthing lugs shall be welded to outside
flange ring diameter of piping flange which connects to pump nozzles.
Earthing lugs shall be made of same material grade than piping
If electrically conductive gaskets cannot be used due to fluid service conditions flange connections shall be
bridged by cable. The cable bridge shall be attached to welded lugs located at piping flange (outside diameter
Location and orientation of welded earthing lugs will be specified by the electrical supervisor on site (field
routing). Specification during design and indication on isometric drawing is not required.
Recommended arrangements for safety device inlet, discharge piping.
Safety valves which blow off into a relief header shall be installed above the header at high point. The outlet line
shall be free of pockets to allow drainage into the relief header.
Safety valves shall be accessible from grade or fixed platform and arrangement shall permit valve removal.
Conventional spring loaded safety valves shall be installed in vertical position, i.e. the inlet flow vertical upward.

text.b: Open systems for gas or vapor services and flashing liquids

page.text: Discharge lines to atmosphere shall be routed vertically up to safe location so that the fluid is dispersed easily.
Outlet shall not be protected by rain hoods, 180° elbows, or bird meshes, etc.
To prevent accumulation of liquids these lines shall be provided with a 10 mm weep hole at the low point for
draining. Please see also special requirements on toxic or flammable services below.

text.b: Open systems for non-flashing liquids, e.g. cooling water

page.text: Discharge lines shall be routed downwards to safe location.

text.b: Safe location for gas or vapor discharge and flashing liquids

page.text: Safe location for gas or vapor discharge is considered to be least 3 m above the highest platform level, with a
minimum horizontal distance of 7.5 m to the nearest adjacent platform for non-hazardous, non-flammable
For toxic or flammable service, e.g. CH
safe location shall be verified by the assigned HSE-
Engineer. API 521 (para 5.8.2.2) recommends that hydrocarbon relief streams discharged directly to
atmosphere should have their vent stacks located at least 15 m (horizontal distance) from any structures or
equipment which are higher than the discharge point.
Depending on the service and the location of the safety valve it shall be assessed with the HSE engineer, if the
drainage of the open safety valve outlet line (weep hole) may stay permanently open.
Safe location for non-flashing liquids is 300 mm above grade outside of escape-, access- and maintenance
Rupture disks shall be installed close to the system to be protected. Fluid shall be discharged to safe location
Tank safety devices shall be installed close to the equipment to be protected.
Tank safety devices shall be accessible for maintenance.
Safety showers and eye wash stations shall be easily accessible. Typical details are shown in Annex J.
ASME B16.5 - Steel Pipe Flanges and Flanged fitting
ASME B16.9 - Factory made wrought steel butt-welding fittings
ASME B16.11 - Forged steel fitting, socket welding and threaded
ASME B16.28 - Wrought steel butt-welding short radius elbows and returns
ASME B16.34 - Steel valves, Flanged welded and Threaded ends
ASME B16.47 – Large diameter steel flanges
ASME B36.10 - Welded and Seamless wrought steel pipe
OISD 114 - Safe handling of Hazardous chemicals
OISD 117- Fire protection Facilities for Petroleum Depot,Terminal,Pipeline
OISD 118,July2008 - Layouts for oil & gas installations
OISD 150 - Design & Safety requirement for LPG mounded storage facility
OISD 164 - Fire proofing in Oil & Gas industry.
IS -8091- Industrial Plant Layout –Code of Safety.
API RP 520 - Recommended Practice for the Design and Installation of Pressure Relieving

text.b: Clearance of pipes underneath steel beams with and without insulation
Weights and allowable spans of steel pipes
Weights and allowable spans of steel pipes (SI-units)

page.text: Figure 2. Allowable spans of steel pipes

text.b: Weights and allowable support spans for steel piping with water fill and insulation

page.text: 1) q = weight per meter of fluid and pipe
= weight per meter of fluid / medium (water)
It is recommended to not exceed a span of 10.0 m to achieve a more balanced load distribution on structures.
Support span for above-ground lines of sprinkler systems shall be acc. to NFPA 13, table 9.2.2.1(a)

text.b: Orientation of piping at columns, vertical vessels and tanks

page.text: Figure 3. Connection to pipe rack. orientation of pipes. manholes and ladders
Figure 4. Arrangement of steam trap assembly
All piping components shall be in compliance with the piping specification and the PID.
Condensate lines shall be supported off column. Stanchion supports placed at grade shall not be used!
Figure 6. Utility station arrangement at platforms
Common insulation with water line in case freeze protection is required.
Rubber hose with NPS ½ ” adaptor suitable for quick release clamp type coupling.
service fitted with ¾” Flange acc. to ASME B16.5
Rubber hose fitted with ¾”claw couplings.
Collection of used water by a collection tray with return to drainage system shall be provided only in closed
